reasoning.correct: Performance uses latency tables to route to the best-performing endpoint for the user.

why_others_wrong.1: Geographic maps regions to endpoints manually, not based on latency.
why_others_wrong.2: Weighted disregards location/latency.
why_others_wrong.3: Priority is for failover, not proximity.

reference.title: Traffic Manager routing methods - Azure Traffic Manager

questions.question: Which authentication methods are supported for Point-to-Site VPN? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Certificate, Azure AD (OpenVPN), and RADIUS are supported. Plain username/password and native smart card support are not direct options.

reasoning.correct: P2S supports certs, Azure AD with OpenVPN, and RADIUS integration.

why_others_wrong.3: Direct username/password is not supported without Azure AD or RADIUS.
why_others_wrong.4: Smart cards can be part of RADIUS but are not a native P2S method.

reference.title: About Point-to-Site VPN - Azure VPN Gateway

questions.question: A VM does not auto-register in a linked private DNS zone with auto-registration enabled. Most likely cause?
options: DNS resolution not enabled on the VNet link
Private zone conflicts with public zone
questions.explanation: Both auto-registration and DNS resolution must be enabled on the VNet link.

reasoning.correct: Without resolution enabled, auto-registration will not function properly.

why_others_wrong.1: Static IPs can still auto-register.
why_others_wrong.2: Private zones can have same names without conflict in VNet scope.
why_others_wrong.3: Auto-registration is VNet-wide, not subnet-restricted.

reference.title: Azure Private DNS zones - Azure DNS

questions.question: To obtain detailed attack metrics and mitigation reports with DDoS Protection Standard, what should you configure?
options: DDoS diagnostic logs in Azure Monitor
questions.explanation: DDoS Protection emits detailed telemetry via diagnostic logs and metrics in Azure Monitor.

reasoning.correct: Enable DDoSProtectionNotifications, MitigationReports, and FlowLogs for detailed insights.

why_others_wrong.1: NSG flow logs do not include DDoS mitigation metrics.
why_others_wrong.2: Firewall logs do not cover platform DDoS telemetry.
why_others_wrong.3: Defender for Cloud alerts are complementary, not the primary telemetry source.

reference.title: Azure DDoS Protection Standard - Azure DDoS Protection

questions.question: You need SSL termination at Application Gateway and re-encryption to backends that use different certificates. What should you configure?
options: End-to-end SSL with multiple backend HTTP settings
questions.explanation: Use end-to-end SSL and separate backend HTTP settings to validate different backend certs.

reasoning.correct: Multiple backend HTTP settings allow associating distinct trusted root certificates per backend pool.

why_others_wrong.0: Passthrough disables inspection and termination at the gateway.
why_others_wrong.2: Termination only sends HTTP to backends, which is not secure.
why_others_wrong.3: Redirect does not address end-to-end encryption.

reference.title: Application Gateway SSL termination and end-to-end SSL

questions.question: Traffic is not flowing through an NVA deployed in a hub VNet. What is typically missing?
options: IP forwarding on the NVA NIC and UDRs to direct traffic
questions.explanation: Enable IP forwarding and route traffic to the NVA with UDRs; public IP is not required for east-west.

reasoning.correct: Azure drops packets not destined for the VM unless IP forwarding is enabled; UDRs steer traffic via the NVA.

why_others_wrong.1: Public IP is not needed for intra-Azure inspection.
why_others_wrong.2: BGP is not necessary for basic hub-spoke inspection.
why_others_wrong.3: DNS settings do not affect L3 forwarding.

reference.title: Network virtual appliances in Azure

questions.question: You require the fastest global failover with health probes. Which service should you use?
questions.explanation: Front Door operates at the edge with anycast and fast health-based failover (<30s).

reasoning.correct: Edge-based anycast plus health probes yield rapid failover to healthy backends globally.

why_others_wrong.0: Traffic Manager is DNS-based and subject to TTL delays.
why_others_wrong.2: Cross-region LB does not provide global HTTP routing semantics like Front Door.
why_others_wrong.3: App Gateway is regional only.

reference.title: Azure Front Door overview

questions.question: You want on-prem locations connected by different ExpressRoute circuits to communicate via Microsoft backbone. What should you enable?
questions.explanation: Global Reach connects ER circuits to enable private on-prem to on-prem connectivity through Microsoft network.

reasoning.correct: Global Reach stitches ER circuits for branch-to-branch via Microsoft backbone.

why_others_wrong.0: Premium increases limits but does not provide branch-to-branch.
why_others_wrong.2: VNet peering is for Azure VNets, not on-prem interconnect.
why_others_wrong.3: VPN uses internet, not the Microsoft backbone.

reference.title: ExpressRoute Global Reach overview

questions.question: Multiple VMs need outbound internet with stable public IP and no inbound exposure. What should you deploy?
options: Azure NAT Gateway associated to the subnet
questions.explanation: NAT Gateway provides scalable outbound SNAT with stable public IPs and no inbound.

reasoning.correct: Designed for outbound-only scenarios, simpler and cheaper than Firewall.

why_others_wrong.0: Public IPs expose VMs to inbound traffic.
why_others_wrong.1: Outbound rules work but are more complex and not as scalable as NAT GW.
why_others_wrong.3: Firewall is overkill and more expensive for simple outbound.

reference.title: Azure NAT Gateway overview

questions.question: You must eliminate public access to Azure SQL Database and connect privately from a VNet. What should you use?
questions.explanation: Private Link provides a private endpoint and allows disabling public network access entirely.

reasoning.correct: Private endpoint places a NIC with private IP in your subnet for SQL Database.

why_others_wrong.0: Service endpoints still require public endpoint on the service.
why_others_wrong.2: SQL Database is a PaaS service, not a VNet resource to peer.
why_others_wrong.3: App Gateway is L7 HTTP/S, not for database protocols.

reference.title: Azure Private Link for SQL Database

questions.question: You are planning a virtual network that must support approximately 3636 virtual machines across multiple subnets with room for growth. Which address space should you choose to minimize waste while allowing subnetting?
questions.explanation: A /18 provides 16382 usable IPs, providing headroom for ~3636 VMs and future growth.

reasoning.correct: Choose the smallest prefix that satisfies current and near-term capacity while allowing subnet segmentation. /19 ≈ 8190 usable, /18 ≈ 16382 usable.

why_others_wrong.0: /21 is too small for the requirement and leaves no room for subnetting or growth.
why_others_wrong.1: /20 may be tight for multi-subnet growth and could constrain future scaling.
why_others_wrong.2: /19 may be insufficient for the upper end of the requirement.

reference.title: Plan virtual networks - Azure Virtual Network

questions.question: You have an NSG with an allow rule for HTTPS (443) from Internet at priority 200. You must block HTTPS from 203.0.174.91 without affecting other sources. What should you do?
options: Create a deny HTTPS rule for the IP with priority 100
Create a deny HTTPS rule for the IP with priority 300
Edit the existing rule to exclude the IP
Add a deny rule at priority 4096
questions.explanation: NSG rules are processed by ascending priority. A lower-number (higher priority) deny must precede the general allow rule to be effective.

reasoning.correct: Place a specific deny with a lower priority number (e.g., 100) so it is evaluated before the broad allow at 200.

why_others_wrong.1: A rule at 300 would be evaluated after the allow at 200 and would never match.
why_others_wrong.2: NSG rules cannot exclude specific IPs within a single rule; you need a separate rule.
why_others_wrong.3: Priority 4096 runs after most custom rules and would not override an earlier allow.

reference.title: Network security groups - Azure Virtual Network

questions.question: Which features are supported by Standard Load Balancer? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Standard Load Balancer supports AZs, multiple frontends, HTTPS probes, and outbound rules. Port 0 wildcard is not supported.

reasoning.correct: Premium SKU capabilities include zone redundancy, multiple frontends, layer-7 compatible probe protocol HTTPS, and egress control via outbound rules.

why_others_wrong.3: Port 0 is not a valid wildcard in Azure Load Balancer; explicit ports or ranges are required.

reference.title: Standard Load Balancer overview - Azure Load Balancer

questions.question: Your on-premises device supports IKEv2 and BGP. Which Azure VPN Gateway configuration provides flexibility and performance?
questions.explanation: Route-based with BGP provides dynamic routing, multiple tunnels, and best flexibility. Basic lacks BGP.

reasoning.correct: Route-based VPN with BGP enables dynamic routing, active-active, and multi-tunnel topologies.

why_others_wrong.0: Basic does not support BGP and has lower throughput.
why_others_wrong.2: Policy-based VPN is less flexible than route-based and lacks certain scenarios.
why_others_wrong.3: Combining policy-based with BGP is not a valid approach for Azure; BGP requires route-based.

reference.title: VPN Gateway design - Azure VPN Gateway

questions.question: What is the correct sequence to map a custom domain to Azure App Service managed by Azure DNS?
options: Create TXT for verification → Add domain → Create CNAME
Add domain → Create CNAME → Create TXT
Create CNAME → Add domain → Create TXT
Create TXT → Create CNAME → Add domain
questions.explanation: Verify ownership first with TXT, then add domain, then route with CNAME.

reasoning.correct: Ownership verification via TXT must precede adding the domain to App Service; traffic routing via CNAME comes last.

why_others_wrong.1: Adding the domain fails if verification is not in place.
why_others_wrong.2: CNAME without verified ownership will not complete the mapping.
why_others_wrong.3: CNAME before adding the domain is premature.

reference.title: Map an existing custom DNS name to Azure App Service

questions.question: Which statements about VNet peering are correct? (Select all that apply)
options: NSG rules apply to peered traffic
Global VNet peering enables cross-region peering
Traffic is encrypted by default between peers
VNets must be in the same subscription
questions.explanation: Peering is non-transitive; NSGs apply; global peering enables cross-region. Traffic is private but not encrypted; cross-subscription peering is supported.

reasoning.correct: VNet peering is private, non-transitive, with NSG enforcement; global peering supports cross-region.

why_others_wrong.3: Traffic uses Microsoft backbone but is not encrypted by default.
why_others_wrong.4: Cross-subscription peering is supported with proper permissions.

reference.title: Virtual network peering - Azure Virtual Network

questions.question: Azure Firewall has DNAT (prio 100), Network (200), and Application (300) rule collections. A packet matches all three. Which is applied?
questions.explanation: DNAT is processed first; once matched, processing stops.

reasoning.correct: Rule processing is hierarchical: DNAT → Network → Application; first match wins.

why_others_wrong.1: Network rules are evaluated after DNAT.
why_others_wrong.2: Application rules are last.
why_others_wrong.3: Azure Firewall does not combine multiple rule types per packet.

reference.title: Azure Firewall rule processing logic

questions.question: Which Application Gateway capabilities support advanced routing? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Path-based, multi-site, and URL rewrite are supported. Arbitrary header or IP-based routing are not standard.

reasoning.correct: App Gateway operates at L7 with host/path-based rules and rewrite; arbitrary header or IP-based routing is not provided as a first-class routing criterion.

why_others_wrong.3: Header inspection exists but routing on arbitrary headers is not a native capability.
why_others_wrong.4: App Gateway routes on HTTP/S properties, not source IP.

reference.title: Application Gateway features - Azure Application Gateway

questions.question: A subnet has system 0.0.0.0/0 → Internet, UDR 10.1.1.0/24 → VNet Gateway, and BGP 10.1.1.0/25 → ExpressRoute. Where is 10.1.1.10 routed?
questions.explanation: Among overlapping routes of same/higher precedence, the longest prefix wins. BGP 10.1.1.0/25 is more specific than UDR 10.1.1.0/24.

reasoning.correct: Precedence: UDR > BGP > System; within same precedence, longest prefix match. Here, /25 beats /24.

why_others_wrong.0: Default route is lowest precedence and least specific.
why_others_wrong.1: UDR /24 is less specific than BGP /25.
why_others_wrong.3: No UDR points to a virtual appliance in this scenario.

reference.title: Virtual network traffic routing - Azure Virtual Network

questions.question: With Microsoft peering, which Azure services are reachable?
options: PaaS services via public endpoints (Storage, SQL, Cosmos DB)
questions.explanation: Microsoft peering exposes Microsoft public services; private endpoints use private peering.

reasoning.correct: Microsoft peering is for public IP-based services (Azure public services) over private connectivity.

why_others_wrong.0: VNet resources use private peering.
why_others_wrong.2: Scope is global for Microsoft public services.
why_others_wrong.3: Private endpoints are reachable via private peering, not Microsoft peering.

reference.title: ExpressRoute routing requirements - Azure ExpressRoute

questions.question: Which are true about Application Security Groups (ASGs)? (Select all that apply)
options: VMs can be in multiple ASGs
ASGs can be used in NSG rules as source/destination
questions.explanation: ASGs are per-VNet constructs used within NSGs and reduce rule sprawl. They do not span VNets or include on-prem IPs.

reasoning.correct: ASGs enable application-centric rule targeting within a VNet; membership is per-VM and can be multiple.

why_others_wrong.2: ASGs are scoped to a single VNet.
why_others_wrong.4: ASGs cannot include external/on-prem IP addresses.

reference.title: Application security groups - Azure Virtual Network

questions.question: Service endpoints are enabled on a subnet. Access to a Storage account that allows only that subnet fails. What is most likely missing?
options: Allow the subnet on the storage firewall rules
Enable service endpoints on the storage account
Custom route to the storage account
questions.explanation: You must add the specific subnet to the service firewall allowlist on the service side.

reasoning.correct: Service endpoints require subnet enablement and service firewall configuration to permit that subnet.

why_others_wrong.1: Endpoints are configured on subnets, not on the service resource.
why_others_wrong.2: Service endpoints work with private IPs; no public IP needed.
why_others_wrong.3: Routing changes are not required for service endpoints.

questions.explanation: Both auto-registration and DNS resolution must be enabled on the VNet link.

reasoning.correct: Without resolution enabled, auto-registration will not function properly.

why_others_wrong.1: Static IPs can still auto-register.
why_others_wrong.2: Private zones can have same names without conflict in VNet scope.
why_others_wrong.3: Auto-registration is VNet-wide, not subnet-restricted.

reference.title: Azure Private DNS zones - Azure DNS

questions.question: To obtain detailed attack metrics and mitigation reports with DDoS Protection Standard, what should you configure?
options: DDoS diagnostic logs in Azure Monitor
questions.explanation: DDoS Protection emits detailed telemetry via diagnostic logs and metrics in Azure Monitor.

reasoning.correct: Enable DDoSProtectionNotifications, MitigationReports, and FlowLogs for detailed insights.

why_others_wrong.1: NSG flow logs do not include DDoS mitigation metrics.
why_others_wrong.2: Firewall logs do not cover platform DDoS telemetry.
why_others_wrong.3: Defender for Cloud alerts are complementary, not the primary telemetry source.

reference.title: Azure DDoS Protection Standard - Azure DDoS Protection

questions.question: You need SSL termination at Application Gateway and re-encryption to backends that use different certificates. What should you configure?
options: End-to-end SSL with multiple backend HTTP settings
questions.explanation: Use end-to-end SSL and separate backend HTTP settings to validate different backend certs.

reasoning.correct: Multiple backend HTTP settings allow associating distinct trusted root certificates per backend pool.

why_others_wrong.0: Passthrough disables inspection and termination at the gateway.
why_others_wrong.2: Termination only sends HTTP to backends, which is not secure.
why_others_wrong.3: Redirect does not address end-to-end encryption.

reference.title: Application Gateway SSL termination and end-to-end SSL

questions.question: Traffic is not flowing through an NVA deployed in a hub VNet. What is typically missing?
options: IP forwarding on the NVA NIC and UDRs to direct traffic
questions.explanation: Enable IP forwarding and route traffic to the NVA with UDRs; public IP is not required for east-west.

reasoning.correct: Azure drops packets not destined for the VM unless IP forwarding is enabled; UDRs steer traffic via the NVA.

why_others_wrong.1: Public IP is not needed for intra-Azure inspection.
why_others_wrong.2: BGP is not necessary for basic hub-spoke inspection.
why_others_wrong.3: DNS settings do not affect L3 forwarding.

reference.title: Network virtual appliances in Azure

questions.question: You have a virtual network with address space 10.0.0.0/16. You need to create a subnet that can accommodate 500 IP addresses while leaving room for future subnets. What subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: A /22 subnet provides 1022 usable IP addresses, which accommodates the 500 required IPs while leaving room for future growth and additional subnets.

reasoning.correct: /22 provides sufficient IP addresses (1022) for current needs (500) while being efficient with address space, allowing for additional subnets in the /16 space.

why_others_wrong.1: /23 provides only 510 IPs, barely meeting requirements with no room for growth.
why_others_wrong.2: /24 provides only 254 IPs, insufficient for the 500 requirement.
why_others_wrong.3: /21 provides too many IPs (2046), wasting address space that could be used for other subnets.

reference.title: Virtual network planning

questions.question: Case Study: Fabrikam needs to store 10TB of infrequently accessed compliance data for 7 years. The data must be available within 15 hours if needed for audits. Cost optimization is critical. Which storage solution should you recommend?
options: Premium SSD with Cool access tier
Standard HDD with Hot access tier
Standard LRS with Archive access tier
Premium Files with Standard access tier
questions.explanation: Archive access tier provides the lowest cost for long-term storage of infrequently accessed data, with retrieval times that meet the 15-hour requirement.

reasoning.correct: Archive tier offers the lowest storage costs for rarely accessed data and provides retrieval within hours, meeting both cost and compliance requirements.

why_others_wrong.0: Premium SSD with Cool tier is expensive for long-term archival storage.
why_others_wrong.1: Hot tier is designed for frequently accessed data and is more expensive for archival scenarios.
why_others_wrong.3: Premium Files is the most expensive option and unnecessary for archival data.

reference.title: Azure Blob storage access tiers

reference.title: Azure Blob storage access tiers

questions.question: You have a virtual network with address space 10.0.0.0/16. You need to create a subnet that can accommodate 500 IP addresses while leaving room for future subnets. What subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: A /22 subnet provides 1022 usable IP addresses, which accommodates the 500 required IPs while leaving room for future growth and additional subnets.

reasoning.correct: /22 provides sufficient IP addresses (1022) for current needs (500) while being efficient with address space, allowing for additional subnets in the /16 space.

why_others_wrong.1: /23 provides only 510 IPs, barely meeting requirements with no room for growth.
why_others_wrong.2: /24 provides only 254 IPs, insufficient for the 500 requirement.
why_others_wrong.3: /21 provides too many IPs (2046), wasting address space that could be used for other subnets.

reference.title: Virtual network planning

questions.question: Case Study: Fabrikam needs to store 10TB of infrequently accessed compliance data for 7 years. The data must be available within 15 hours if needed for audits. Cost optimization is critical. Which storage solution should you recommend?
options: Premium SSD with Cool access tier
Standard HDD with Hot access tier
Standard LRS with Archive access tier
Premium Files with Standard access tier
questions.explanation: Archive access tier provides the lowest cost for long-term storage of infrequently accessed data, with retrieval times that meet the 15-hour requirement.

reasoning.correct: Archive tier offers the lowest storage costs for rarely accessed data and provides retrieval within hours, meeting both cost and compliance requirements.

reasoning.correct: /22 provides sufficient IP addresses (1022) for current needs (500) while being efficient with address space, allowing for additional subnets in the /16 space.

why_others_wrong.1: /23 provides only 510 IPs, barely meeting requirements with no room for growth.
why_others_wrong.2: /24 provides only 254 IPs, insufficient for the 500 requirement.
why_others_wrong.3: /21 provides too many IPs (2046), wasting address space that could be used for other subnets.

reference.title: Virtual network planning

questions.question: Case Study: Fabrikam needs to store 10TB of infrequently accessed compliance data for 7 years. The data must be available within 15 hours if needed for audits. Cost optimization is critical. Which storage solution should you recommend?
options: Premium SSD with Cool access tier
Standard HDD with Hot access tier
Standard LRS with Archive access tier
Premium Files with Standard access tier
questions.explanation: Archive access tier provides the lowest cost for long-term storage of infrequently accessed data, with retrieval times that meet the 15-hour requirement.

reasoning.correct: Archive tier offers the lowest storage costs for rarely accessed data and provides retrieval within hours, meeting both cost and compliance requirements.

why_others_wrong.0: Premium SSD with Cool tier is expensive for long-term archival storage.
why_others_wrong.1: Hot tier is designed for frequently accessed data and is more expensive for archival scenarios.
why_others_wrong.3: Premium Files is the most expensive option and unnecessary for archival data.

reference.title: Azure Blob storage access tiers

questions.question: Case Study: Fabrikam needs to store 10TB of infrequently accessed compliance data for 7 years. The data must be available within 15 hours if needed for audits. Cost optimization is critical. Which storage solution should you recommend?
options: Premium SSD with Cool access tier

questions.explanation: Archive access tier provides the lowest cost for long-term storage of infrequently accessed data, with retrieval times that meet the 15-hour requirement.

questions.question: You have a virtual network with address space 10.0.0.0/16. You need to create a subnet that can accommodate 500 IP addresses while leaving room for future subnets. What subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: A /22 subnet provides 1022 usable IP addresses, which accommodates the 500 required IPs while leaving room for future growth and additional subnets.

reasoning.correct: /22 provides sufficient IP addresses (1022) for current needs (500) while being efficient with address space, allowing for additional subnets in the /16 space.

why_others_wrong.1: /23 provides only 510 IPs, barely meeting requirements with no room for growth.
why_others_wrong.2: /24 provides only 254 IPs, insufficient for the 500 requirement.
why_others_wrong.3: /21 provides too many IPs (2046), wasting address space that could be used for other subnets.

questions.question: You have a virtual network with address space 10.0.0.0/16. You need to create a subnet that can accommodate 500 IP addresses while leaving room for future subnets. What subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: A /22 subnet provides 1022 usable IP addresses, which accommodates the 500 required IPs while leaving room for future growth and additional subnets.

reasoning.correct: /22 provides sufficient IP addresses (1022) for current needs (500) while being efficient with address space, allowing for additional subnets in the /16 space.

why_others_wrong.1: /23 provides only 510 IPs, barely meeting requirements with no room for growth.
why_others_wrong.2: /24 provides only 254 IPs, insufficient for the 500 requirement.
why_others_wrong.3: /21 provides too many IPs (2046), wasting address space that could be used for other subnets.

reference.title: Virtual network planning

questions.question: Case Study: Fabrikam needs to store 10TB of infrequently accessed compliance data for 7 years. The data must be available within 15 hours if needed for audits. Cost optimization is critical. Which storage solution should you recommend?
options: Premium SSD with Cool access tier
Standard HDD with Hot access tier
Standard LRS with Archive access tier
Premium Files with Standard access tier
questions.explanation: Archive access tier provides the lowest cost for long-term storage of infrequently accessed data, with retrieval times that meet the 15-hour requirement.

reasoning.correct: Archive tier offers the lowest storage costs for rarely accessed data and provides retrieval within hours, meeting both cost and compliance requirements.

why_others_wrong.0: Premium SSD with Cool tier is expensive for long-term archival storage.
why_others_wrong.1: Hot tier is designed for frequently accessed data and is more expensive for archival scenarios.
why_others_wrong.3: Premium Files is the most expensive option and unnecessary for archival data.

certificationInfo.title: Microsoft Certified: Azure Administrator Associate
certificationInfo.description: This certification validates your expertise in implementing, managing, and monitoring an organization's Microsoft Azure environment, including virtual networks, storage, compute, identity, security, and governance.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of operating systems, networking, servers, and virtualization
Experience with PowerShell or Azure CLI
Knowledge of Azure services and workloads, security, and governance

skills: Manage Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) objects
Manage role-based access control (RBAC)
Manage Azure AD Privileged Identity Management (PIM)
Manage Azure AD Identity Protection
Manage subscriptions and governance
Manage storage accounts
Manage data in Azure Storage
Configure Azure Files and Azure Blob Storage
Manage storage with tools such as Storage Explorer and AzCopy
Configure storage replication, backup, and recovery

skillsMeasured.category: Deploy and manage Azure compute resources (20-25%)
skills: Configure VMs for high availability and scalability
Automate deployment and configuration of VMs
Manage VM extensions and custom script extensions
Create and configure containers and Azure Container Instances
Manage Azure App Service and App Service plans
Create and configure virtual networks, subnets, and peering
Configure private and public IP addressing
Configure network security groups and Azure Firewall
Configure Azure Load Balancer and Application Gateway
Configure Azure DNS and custom domains

skillsMeasured.category: Monitor and back up Azure resources (10-15%)
skills: Monitor resources by using Azure Monitor
Configure diagnostic settings and log analytics
Configure Azure Backup and Azure Site Recovery
Configure alerts and action groups
Monitor costs and optimize spending
Configure and manage Azure Policy
Configure and manage Azure Blueprints
Configure and manage resource locks
Configure and manage tags
Configure and manage cost management

studyResources.description: Official Microsoft learning path with modules covering all exam objectives
